title: Performance Statements
description: You can't control your thoughts. But you can answer them.

blocks.content: Five words can change a race. Not a mantra or a prayer. Five specific words, chosen in advance, rehearsed until automatic, deployed at the exact moment your brain tries to shut you down.

Eight minutes into a climb. Legs burning. The chimp starts screaming: "I can't hold this." "This is too hard." Every sentence pulls your attention away from what you need to DO and drowns you in what you FEEL. Now imagine the same climb — except this time you have an answer. "Hips forward, light hands." Five words. Technical. Action-directed. Your hips tilt forward. Your death grip releases. The chimp screamed, and you answered.
By the end of this lesson, you will have three memorized Performance Statements — one technical, one pain response, one process.

blocks.question: The Teammate Test helps you counter:
blocks.explanation: Correct. The Teammate Test catches the double standard — brutal self-talk after a bad session that you'd never aim at someone you respect. It's the antidote to perfectionistic self-punishment.

blocks.content: You can't control your thoughts. But you can answer them.

Imagine a chessboard. Your thoughts are the chess pieces — black and white. "I can't do this" is a black rook. "Keep pushing" is a white queen. You are not the chess pieces. You are the chessboard. You can't stop the black rook from appearing. But you can deploy the white queen. Performance Statements don't suppress negative thoughts — they replace them. The chimp can scream all it wants. You've already moved.

tabs.label: Technical
tabs.content: Addresses execution — what your body should be doing. An instructional cue, two to five words, focused on a specific physical action. Think about what breaks down in your form when effort gets high. "Hips forward, light hands." "Quick feet, tall spine." "Elbows in, shoulders down." If you say the words and nothing changes in your body, they're too vague.

tabs.label: Pain Response
tabs.content: Addresses suffering — the moment the chimp says the effort is too much. Grounded in something concrete, not cheerleading. "If you keep moving, you'll get there." "You've hurt worse than this and finished." "The next checkpoint. That's all." It should sound like something a grizzled, honest coach would say at mile 80. Calm truth, not hype.

tabs.label: Process
tabs.content: Addresses the days when motivation is gone — not race day, but Tuesday afternoon when you don't want to start. "Something is better than nothing." "Today's session is tomorrow's evidence." "You don't need to feel like it. You need to do it." This statement keeps you in the game when the game doesn't feel worth playing.

items.title: Hatzigeorgiadis et al. (2011) — 32-Study Meta-Analysis
items.content: Self-talk works. Consistently. Effect size of 0.48 across 32 studies. Instructional self-talk (technique cues like "hips forward") had a larger effect size (0.67) for fine motor tasks. Motivational self-talk ("keep going") was stronger for endurance and strength tasks. The type of statement matters — match it to the type of challenge.

items.title: Planned vs. Reactive Self-Talk
items.content: Reactive self-talk is whatever the chimp generates in the moment — usually catastrophic. Planned self-talk is pre-built and rehearsed. The research is clear: planned self-talk outperforms reactive self-talk because it's already loaded when you need it. You don't have to think of the right thing to say under duress. It's memorized. It fires automatically.

items.title: Third-Person Self-Talk for Distance
items.content: Cognitive defusion — from Steven Hayes's ACT framework (1999) — explains the mechanism. A negative thought goes from "truth" to "noise" the instant you deploy a counter-statement. Third-person self-talk (Kross et al., 2014) — using your own name: "Matt, hips forward" — creates additional psychological distance, making it easier to observe thoughts as events rather than facts.

items.label: Write one Technical statement
items.detail: What breaks down in your form when effort gets high? Write the correction in five words or fewer. This cue should immediately produce a physical action.

items.label: Write one Pain Response statement
items.detail: What does the chimp say when it wants you to quit? Write the honest counter-move. Not "you can do it" — something concrete. A fact about your preparation or the process.

items.label: Write one Process statement
items.detail: What do you tell yourself on the days you don't want to start? Write the phrase that gets you out the door when motivation is zero.

items.label: Memorize all three
items.detail: Say them out loud. Repeat until they're automatic. In the heat of effort, you won't remember a paragraph. Five words or fewer. Burned into memory.

items.label: Deploy when the chess pieces appear
items.detail: In your next hard session, when the chimp fires, answer it. Out loud if alone. Under your breath if in a group. Every time. Until the response is as automatic as the negative thought.

blocks.content: Your commitment: My 3 Performance Statements — Technical: ___. Pain: ___. Process: ___. (Write them now. Memorize them today.)
Memorize them. Deploy them. The chimp talks first — you get the last word.
Next — defining who you are as an athlete, on purpose.
